steps.reveal: Tanta vis = so great a force (nom.)

hints.accusative: Accusative of 'vis' would be 'vim' — 'vis' here is nominative singular, the subject
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'vi' — 'vis' is nominative singular
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'vis' in plural 'virium' — here 'vis' is nominative subject

steps.prompt: What declension is 'vis' and what is unusual about it?
steps.reveal: vis = force (nom.); irregular: vis, —, —, vim, vi; pl. vires, virium...

hints.3rd declension, regular: 'Vis' is 3rd declension but highly irregular — it lacks genitive/dative singular
hints.2nd declension: 'Vis' does not follow 2nd declension patterns — it is an irregular 3rd declension noun
hints.4th declension: 'Vis' is not 4th declension — it is an irregular 3rd declension noun

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'scelus' — 'sceleris' with -is is genitive singular
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'scelere' — 'sceleris' with -is is genitive singular

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'sceleris' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'sceleris' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'sceleris' ends in -is

steps.reveal: in unum me = against me alone (acc. with 'in')

hints.nominative: Nominative would not follow 'in' — 'in' + accusative means 'against'
hints.dative: 'In' does not govern the dative — it takes accusative (motion/hostility) or ablative (location)

steps.prompt: What is the function of 'in' + accusative here?

hints.location within: 'In' + ablative expresses location — 'in' + accusative expresses motion toward or hostility
hints.purpose: Purpose clauses use 'ut' or 'ad' — 'in' + accusative here means 'against'
hints.accompaniment: 'Cum' + ablative expresses accompaniment — 'in' + accusative here is hostile

steps.prompt: What is the function of the reflexive 'se'?
steps.target: direct object referring back to the subject 'vis'
choices: direct object referring back to the subject 'vis'

hints.indirect object: Indirect object would be 'sibi' — 'se' is accusative, direct object of 'convertit'
hints.subject of an infinitive: There is no indirect discourse here — 'se' is simply reflexive with 'convertit'
hints.ablative reflexive: Ablative would be 'se' with a preposition — here 'se' is accusative object of 'convertit'

hints.to come together: 'To come together' is 'convenire' — 'convertit' comes from 'convertere' (to turn)

hints.present indicative: 'Convertit' can be present or perfect — context of the narrative makes it perfect here
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'convertet' — 'convertit' is present or perfect indicative
hints.perfect subjunctive: Perfect subjunctive would be 'converterit' — 'convertit' is perfect indicative

hints.verto, vertere, verti, versum: These are the parts of 'vertere' (to turn) — the base verb without the con- prefix
hints.convenio, convenire, conveni, conventum: These are the parts of 'convenire' (to come together) — different compound
hints.confero, conferre, contuli, collatum: These are the parts of 'conferre' (to bring together) — entirely different verb

steps.prompt: What does 'Tanta' indicate about the degree of force?

hints.interrogative: 'how great?': 'Quanta' is interrogative — 'tanta' is the demonstrative/correlative 'so great'
hints.relative: 'as great as': 'Quanta' serves as relative — 'tanta' is the correlative demonstrative

translation: So great a force of crime turned itself against me alone
